title: 2025/10/29 Primary Production subcommittee A
displayTitle: 2025-10-29 - Primary Production Committee - Primary Production [Subcommittee A]
description: Wednesday, 29 October 2025 | Primary Production Animal Welfare (Regulations for Management of Pigs) Amendment Bill
descriptionSummary: Primary Production. Animal Welfare (Regulations for Management of Pigs) Amendment Bill

chunks.text: Well, let's go and hear from Pat.

You could just let me know when Pat's online so I can start proceeding. Izzy, that would be brilliant. Of course. It's coming through now. And we have Pat for how many minutes? Five. Yes. Brilliant. Are you there, pat?
Hello? Hello? Hello? Pat, are you there? Pat, you need to accept the promotion to be a panelist in order to speak to the committee.

Do we have you, pat, or are you, uh, still, uh, working through your tech? Um, we've not been able to promote Pat, Mr. She, I'll try one more time. Pat, could you please accept the promotion to be a panelist?
Okay. They've declined. Um, We can move across to our next, um, submitter is Izzy, which is whom? Um, Aaron Macy. We'll move to Aaron Macy now. Very good, Lee. Thank you. Izzy. No trouble.

Gilda. Hello Aaron. How are you sir? It's Mark Cameron. Uh, you're welcome. Welcome to the primary production Select committee. We have Rachel Boyer, again, others, miles Anderson to talk to you today. We have you for five minutes, Aaron. Um, so please lead off with your thoughts on the pork industry and where we take it and, um, and maybe if you leave a bit of time, we've got a few moments for questions at the end.

Cool. Thank you. Um, yeah. Well, my name is Aaron Macy. Um, I reside in Canterbury and I'm a student. Um, I really came on here today to put my face to my submission. I presume you guys have read my submission, so I won't go over all the points. Um, I think this is a really important bill to be discussing, um, and ultimately, well, if he has huge issues with animal welfare standards being upheld, my primary issue is with what this bill does to the rule of law and the, the previous findings of the high courts and ruling current crates were an illegal practice.

Um, I believe that the Animal Welfare Act is very clear in its intentions to promote animal welfare. Uh, and that's not at the promote animal welfare in a way that economic, uh, issues don't overthrow the welfare of animals. Um, yeah. So my submission really is about, uh, this, this bill not encompassing the spirit of the animal welfare Acts 1999, and it using regulatory loopholes to go about subverting the, the process in which a code

of welfare would normally be made. And at the end of the day, while this is making an amendment to the primary acts, is really going about to create a new code of welfare for pigs. Um, given the short amount of time that submissions were open for the select committee, uh, and, and also some of the comments that the, this government has made, uh, when this bill was announced about it being a win for animal welfare, I find completely disingenuous.

It's another example of how the democratic process is being eroded in this country. We rely on the courts to uphold and interpret the laws that the government and the legislator make. We have had that happen in 2020. We had it found that the use of these crates were illegal.

And yeah, I just think it's a, it's, it's kind of disgusting in my opinion. Um, again, not to do with the animal welfare issues, which are real, uh, but for the abhorrent way in which this bill is being passed and, uh, how it signifies, uh, sorry, not signifies.

It's a, another example of legislation being rushed through as a knee jerk reaction and it's not well thought out good law. Mm-hmm. Um, those were my major points. So I'm open to some questions. So, uh, well, I'll lead off Aaron. I'm not quite sure, uh, what my colleagues are currently doing, but I'm sure they'll have several questions.

You say that it's expedited, uh, piece of legislation and by virtue it's, um, it's a dis disingenuous engagement with the constituency. How do you square then, Aaron, that, that there's a regulatory hole in the law, uh, that is being amended under the advice of the minister and his officials in Naac, and I'm sure you are aware who Naac is.

How do you square that? There will be a cessation of one set of regulations before the new ones were implemented, and this, this build change is seeking to fill that hole so there aren't pig farmers with no laws regulating them in this regulatory gap.

So how do you square that? Yeah, so what you've just presented is a false dichotomy. There is not the only two options of going through with the law as it is now, or creating a law that creates suffering in, uh, for pigs for a really long time. There are workarounds. Um, I understand that it's a really, uh, it's a really big thing for the industry to, to make these changes.

And that's why I think that the government should be supporting farmers to make these changes. But at the end of the day, just because this primary industry is struggling, that's not a good enough reason to go against the welfare of animals. And it's actually enshrined in the legislation that economic issues cannot be a reason that animal welfare is pushed down.

Thank you. Aaron, do you have any further questions? Uh, members, Uh, no, but thank you for your submission, Aaron. Really clear points of view. Thank you. Appreciate it. I, I Would like to make one more, um, comment. Yes, please. Briefly, if you could be so kind, Aaron. Thank you. Sweet. Um, yeah, I just, um, I was just looking at your bio mark on your ACT website, and it literally says the challenges to dealing with the government inspectors turning up on the farm.

And I just like to highlight that I understand that your position within the government, but that just seems like a very big conflict of interest to have you, uh, being the chair of this person who is literally making a law amendment about government inspectors coming up and being able to turn up to a farm. Um, Oh, I, I, I just respond to that.

Um, I, I, I don't make decisions on behalf of the committee. A committee makes decisions by resolution, so I have a totally different opinion to your statement, but I appreciate your opinions are your opinions. Uh, I chair a committee, which is very collegial, and we work through issues, uh, by, by majority.

Um, and that just happens to be currently the government. It's often the other way. So thank you for your opinions. Um, I'm, I'm grateful for them. Uh, and, um, I, I, I will leave it to the next, uh, submitter to square up what he thinks and doesn't think so. Thank you, Aaron or she. Thank you very much for your time.

Um, Mr. Chair, we will now try Pat basket again. What we're gonna do is rather than promote to panelists, we'll just allow to speak from the attendee role so you won't be able to see Pat, but ideally should be able to hear That. Thank you, Izzy. Always grateful my, my dear. Thank you so much. Right, thank you.

Hello, pat. Good morning. Welcome to the pri welcome to the Primary Production Select committee. We have you for five minutes, sir. We're very grateful for your time, and I'll leave it with you, and if you would be obliging to leave a few moments for questions. Okay. Thank you. Uh, thank you for this opportunity to persuade you to vote against the animal welfare amendment bill that's currently before the house.

In expressing my concerns about how this bill will perpetuate the inhumane treatment of pigs, I, uh, will put the issue in a wider context. But I'd like to start by asking, how many of you have a cat or a dog at home? Um, if you do, you'll understand the basic needs of animals, like, um, those that you look after in your homes, the wider context population increase worldwide.

And the challenges of climate change mean we have to ask basic questions about the economics of food production. Now, it's not the same world as, uh, when I, or maybe you grew up growing crops to feed animals in order to feed people is poor economics.

When animal agriculture was domestic, meat production was integrated into the local economy. Now, industrialization of, uh, animal agriculture means that most of the maize grown in the US and the Sawyer in Argentina goes to industrialized feedlots for cattle and pigs around the world.

The conditions which these animals are forced to endure make a mockery of minister hoard's attempt to postpone improvements in pigs confinement. He claims that come 2035 New Zealand will have the strictest animal welfare regulations in the world, but that could have been now if the existing law had been allowed to stand.

In the meantime, we will allow pig farmers to confine millions of these intelligent animals for up to 33 days in crates, in which they can only roll over the seven days That will be allowed in 2035. They should be zero and mating stalls. The current seven days becomes three hours.

But you know all this, but what an admission of prolonged in humanity and animal cruelty. Those of you who have pets will also know that if you did to your animals what you condone being done to these pigs, you would be arrested and find. You will also be familiar with the statistics of New Zealand's pork industry and know that not all producers use these appalling methods.

Now, remind you, I think it's approximately 42% of pork is produced on free farms here, but we could have joined those countries who've already banned farrowing crates and m stalls and allowed only free range pig farms. We could have supported those who use em the moment to make these changes.

We could have endorsed this policy by allowing imported pork only from countries with similar bans. And you are familiar with those statistics, how much bacon you eat. And ham here comes from countries where, um, they do not have good animal welfare regulations.

But the ensuing price rises if we had endorsed this policy and spread it around, would've made evident to consumers. The real cost of eating pork in this climate changed world of shrinking resources for water and land meat must become an expensive delicacy. It will, in fact, inevitably become an expensive delicacy.

If we can't afford the full cost of producing it, we shouldn't eat it. Please reject this bill, which reflects the immediate and short-term interest expressed by the industry body. That's all I have to say. Thank you. Very, very, very grateful, pat, for your submission this morning.

Thank you so much for, uh, how, how passionate you are about the industry and where it should take itself. Members. Do we have any questions for Pat this morning? I cannot see anyone. Rachel, do you have a question at all? No. Thank you, pat. Very clear submission and point of view and appreciate it. Thank you.

Thank you, Rachel. Um, there appear to be no questions. Pat, thank you so much for your time. Okay. We've remain very grateful as a, as a committee for varying opinions and how we take this bill forward. So thank you for your time this morning. Okay. And yours, Dear Cheerio, take care. Goodbye. Okay, Mr.

Chair, we now have Sarah Spencer, who is online. Thank you. Thank you, Izzy. Good morning, Sarah. Can we, can you hear the committee and see the committee, uh, at all, Sarah? I can. Good morning. Good morning. Welcome to the pri. Good morning. Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee.

Thank you so much for your time this morning. This morning. We are very grateful for your time. We have you for five minutes. Sarah, if you would keep your introductory remarks, moderately brief, um, I'm sure my, uh, my dear members will have questions for you. Thank you so much. Great Kiara, and good morning to everybody.

Thanks for the opportunity to speak today. Um, I felt it was really important to, um, join in on this conversation. I've followed the issue for years. Um, I am from mid Canterbury originally, grew up in a small rural town. Spent my childhood on farms, worked on farms, um, understand what farming is actually like.

Our school trips to freezing works showed me how meat was produced. I became, um, quite aware from a young age how we get our food. Um, it distressed me as the years went by, um, to see factory farming and the demand, but also balanced. I could see the struggles of the farmer and what that means.

So when I come to this, I come from a really mixed background of understanding this issue from one side of the fence and the country farm and to the other side, becoming an, a member of the safe group when I was 15. It's 42 years later, and I still stand on the steps of parliament with my banners for getting these chickens and pigs out of cages.

So in 2020, there I was feeling great that we were getting somewhere with Gareth Hughes there helping us, um, going to the high court for the decision which we were overjoyed had happened. And I thought for the farming community, this would actually provide at last some clarity of what they could expect to happen.

I knew that this would be a big deal. There is changes for their whole production, their whole farming practice. But for me, when I see an animal in a cage that can't turn around and I see a free range practice, which does take a lot more work and staff, I really get that.

But the product on the world stage puts New Zealand as a country who actually values their animals and how we care for them. That to me, and I've spoken to many farmers on this, and I know how conflicting it can be to try and deal with the challenges they face.

So I just wanted to speak up on that realistic view that I feel we are becoming a culture and a country that doesn't actually get where our bacon comes from in the pack and save shelves. That people are pretty much buying stuff in plastic and not understanding it by speaking up on this issue and seeing a bill that is being very quickly pushed through and it's have to refer to the first submitter, the hour.

And you put that pretty clearly. I'm not feeling that as a very, uh, a trustworthy democratic process either. So I'm speaking on a couple of things there that I'm not happy on that count. And I'd love your committee to be able to go into parliament to recommend that this bill is not put through at all on the grounds of the care of the animals, the, the, the, the process that has been pushed through that I cannot see if I had not received the information on this, I'm so grateful I got the chance to put the submission in.

If I hadn't heard from my good old safe friends that this was an issue to speak up on, I probably wouldn't have known about it. So I think we're getting a small snapshot of people here speaking, but I know there is a huge wider community in New Zealand that would be behind this, and I know they are. So I really just wanted to say that, um, I think it's very important that we show in this country, and it's not popular sometimes to speak up and push back.

And I have, I have seen firsthand what a small group of people can do in a little group to make change. And we can do this on this one. Farmers will have a clear understanding now. It's a bit like the earthquake strengthening. It's been going on for a while. Nobody knew what was happening.

We now will have that, and that ruling should be upheld. So thank you for this opportunity, and I, I hope that, um, we can actually make this change for good. Thank you, Sarah. Thank you for your submission. Uh, members, do we have any questions at all for Sarah while we have her online?

No. Sarah, there appear to be none. Thank you very, very much. Just very quickly, if I can, um, I've got a quick question. If, if time allows me, how do you feel that we square up, um, where we're going as an industry, the difficulties that farmers face, and as you said, it's a very active minority, that seems to be the loudest voice against this, but do you think that people's moral compass is interfering in private business a little too much?

Or is that an accepted reality where people that don't farm tell those that do how they should do it? How do, how do you square that, um, in society, do you think? Very quickly? Yeah, I really get that, um, about people speaking up that have no idea what the average farmer and person is dealing with.

It. It really, um, that's why I felt I had to speak because I have been there. I've mucked out the cages, I've done the work. I've seen what it takes, what I think we need. If here's the problem that's happening in our society, if we don't start on this level and make a change, we will continue to produce food and animals in huge quantities for a demanding population that has no idea where this food is coming from and how these animals are raised.

So there, there isn't a great answer to that question, actually, mark, because I feel that the industry needs to change. It's like David Longe standing up for the nuclear issue. It's, it's time to stand up and make that change. It's like the climate, it's not easy, but we have far too many people demanding a pork and a ham on their tables that they do not understand how they are raised.

They have to be smaller productions. We just shouldn't be morally having animals in cages. And I know the farrowing process and two to three times a year with the litter, and I know that the regulations are coming back down to say, we want these pigs prior to the farrowing and the week after.

I know they're trying to lessen the time in the cages. The reality, if we leave it and, and if we try to ignore this, um, uh, high court ruling, we are going to get farmers who will take it. There will always be some who will push the limits.

And I just feel it's wrong that we have that chance that we are gonna mistreat animals for longer than is necessary. I'm sorry, I don't think I actually answered Properly. No, no, thank you. Loophole for you. But I, I just feel it's unfortunate, but we have to make a stand and I'd like it to be people that understand the background too.

So that would be a way for me, if I could, I could be on a committee that to say that if we could do this from people who understand, rather than people sitting at their desks in Wellington saying, this is what we should be doing. I'd like to have a rural community behind me to say, and there are who think it's wrong. But, um, Yeah. So I don't mean to interrupt. Thank you so much for your passion.

Thank you for answering my question. I, I know it was a difficult one. I hope you have a lovely day and, um, we'll, we'll see, uh, what the next submitters have to say. Uh, have a cracking good day. Thank you so much. Great. Thank you, Cheerio. Take care. Bye Mr. Chair. We now move to Christopher Romy.

Thank you, Izzy. Good morning. Christopher, are you there? Christopher? Good morning. Can you see me and hear me? Yes, sir. We can hear you. I can't see you, but I'm sure my colleagues can thank you so much for your time. We're very grateful for your time this morning.

We have you for five minutes. Oh, there you go. We can see you. Oh, great. Hello. Great. Thank you mate. Great to have you online please. Good Morning. Um, uh, I urge you to, uh, reject this amendment bill. Um, I'd just like to very quickly describe this, my animal shelter here. So if I had to keep sheep and milk, she for seven years to fully understand what the requirements are of, of these animals.

So this space here is a space of about three meters by two meters. And they can come outta the paddock and use this space by choice. They continuously use it. It has a hard floor, and it has a hard roof with three meters of rain. They need it, and they get excited when they come here.

Lambs come here, the milking sheep come here, it's highlight for them. So you can see they're running around inside and playing in here. Now, what if I take this date? Now they're running, if I take this date and I now make this space an enclosed space.

So previously that three meter by two meter space was a wonderful place where they wanted to be in play. What if I enclose this space now? So an animal that's in this space now, it isn't such a good space now. It's actually, um, not such a good place to live. They can't escape from it.
What if we bring this here now, bring it in more?

So now I've made this space even narrower. So in two steps, I've made this shelter from a wonderful place to an abominable place. Um, and so that's basically my submission in brief, why the amendment, uh, should be rejected by the select committee because I consider it to be cruel.

And I spend a lot of time in this batch with these animals, milking these animals, tending these animals. I spend a lot of time thinking about what it's like for other animals that live in confined spaces, and I do findable. So thank you very much. Thank you, Chris. Thank you so much.

What a wonderful, um, submission. I like the, the illustration, uh, and live fire as it, as it, so, great submission, mate. We're very grateful for it. Do we have questions from members for Chris Wa here? Uh, um, just speak up if you've got questions.

Sorry, Tim, I can't see you Wall. No, Chris, there don't seem to be any questions. Again, again, mate, I, I wanna thank you for your submission. We are genuinely very grateful as a committee. We often don't get these kind of, um, in, in-house submissions where we can see actively what people are thinking and how they see it in the real world.

So thank you for your submission. We're genuine, genuinely very grateful for it. Uh, it helps us digest where we take the legislation forward. So thank you again and have a great day. Thank You very much. Cheerio, Chris. Take care. Cheers, Thank you.

Who have we got online now? Izzy? Uh, we now move to Walt Cavendish. Mr. Chair. Oh, Walt. Yes, I know Walt well. Are you there, Walt? I am. And yourself. Good to see you as always. You health healthy? Um, yeah, up down. Well, I'm northern. You're in the white. Always good to see you.

Mark, we have, we have you for five minutes. Thank you so much for your submission. You can take it as read and, um, please well, um, speak your truth as you see it. And, and, and I'm sure we, we'll have many questions for you after the fact. So I'll leave it in your capable hands. Thank you, mark. Thank you.

Look, um, fundamentally I don't support the extending the transition to 2035. I I don't think there's any justification for that. You know, the pork industry's been on notice for, uh, uh, a considerable period of time. And if we look at the poultry industry, uh, where they had the 10 year lead in to get rid of battery cages in New Zealand, um, a lot of it didn't happen until right toward the end.

There was a heck of a rush. There was a shortage of eggs. So the industry had 10 years to get rid of battery cages, and they sat on their hands and did nothing. And I just think that, you know, the pork industry in this country's not that big. Um, there's other examples, for example, in California under proposition, um, 12 where they gave their producers, um, five years, um, to, um, where they put the bill through.

And then it was enforced five years later. And I think in New Zealand here, we really should only be looking at a two year, um, transition, or maybe three at the most, I think would be suitable. Otherwise, people are just gonna sit on their hands and do nothing. And, um, I don't think that's fair on the farmers that have already, uh, made these changes, uh, in their, in their systems.

But I think the fundamental thing is, it doesn't really matter too much on this from my point of view, my real problem is that we are not incentivizing, uh, the pork industry in this country. They are competing against subsidized pork, lower wealthier pork and all these things. And in New Zealand, we say we don't want farrowing crates, we don't want mating stalls and all these sorts of things, which is absolutely wonderful.

It's in line with our animal welfare beliefs that came out in, um, 1999. But while we hold our farmers to the highest standards on animal welfare, and I'm a farmer, you are a farmer, you know, we all have to follow these high animal welfare standards. And we do it, um, because we, uh, we love our animals, but there is a financial cost to this.

And if we are asking our farmers to meet these standards, then we have to be prepared to take some action. And I really think that this particular bill should be tied to something like Steve Abel's, uh, members bill, uh, the animal products closing the welfare gap amendment bill, and have the two go together so that we make sure that what's coming into this country meets the same standards that New Zealand pig farmers are required by law to meet.

And unless we do that, you know, our industry's not going to go anywhere. Uh, we produce some of the best pork in the world, but we are hamstrung because when you go to a supermarket and you get your salamis and your hams and your bacons, it's all from imported pork that don't meet any many of these standards. And I would ask the committee to very seriously consider advising the government to tie the two things together.

Because if we believe in animal welfare, which we do, then the two go hand in hand. And I think that really what's happened here is the ministers decided to put this, this 10 year transition in, uh, to appease the industry. But at the same time, it's beneficial because it stops people like me who have been advocating very firmly to close this welfare gap.

Because by putting the farrowing crate ban out 10 years, the government can argue, well actually, you know, the imports are meeting the same standards as our pig farmers at the, and to me, that's just not right. We, we are actually going backwards in animal, uh, welfare instead of going forwards.

And you as a committee have the ability to, to debate, you know, sort of re re uh, massage this into something that's gonna deliver both for animal welfare, but for this country. And you've got a very, um, serious job to do. And I would ask you to consider, um, putting animal welfare first, but at the same time, remembering that there are farmers out there that this will really affect.

And there has to be a balancing line. But I think 10 year transition is just too long. It throws it way down the road by a decade. And I think we need to be a little bit more, um, aggressive, I think, and, and get that transfer happening, uh, sooner, that transition happening sooner, but at the same time, remembering that our farmers do compete unfairly.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. Always a pleasure, Walt. Uh, you articulate yourself beautifully and, and I'm grateful for your passion. Uh, members, do we have a, uh, yes. Line of questions for Walt? Rachel? I've got A couple, Mr. Chair. Thank you, please. Hi, Walt, Rachel here. Nice to see you. And, um, thanks so much for your advocacy.

Um, I've got a couple of quite specific questions about, um, I I totally take your point around the, um, the connection with, um, international, um, trade and those requirements. It would probably be advised to us that it'll be out of the scope for the bill, but I, I, I take your points.

Um, what I'm, I've got two lines of questioning for you really. The first is, so the 10 year transition period doesn't actually lead to a, a a, a ban in farrowing crates. It would just change some, um, rules around, uh, how they can be used and for how long and their size and things.

I'm quite interested in your thoughts on farrowing crates and in in general. Um, because as a committee, we are hearing two quite different points of view from, uh, people across the community. So from, um, advisors from, uh, animal welfare rights organizations and from, um, uh, uh, from farmers as well, uh, about whether they are good or bad for pig and piglet welfare.

So I'm, I'm actually genuinely interested in your thoughts on the use and what you would see as the best way to ensure animal welfare, um, uh, for pigs and piglets, just generally. I think, um, I think seven days is a, is a balanced, is a balanced number because I mean, whether you do or whether you don't, you're either gonna have a higher piglet mortality rate by getting rid of them entirely or by having a longer faring period.

You may have your piglet a less piglet death, but you're gonna have a sour that's, that's restrained for a longer period of time. So there has to be a balance. And I think seven days seems reasonable to me. And some of the farmers I've talked to, um, have said, well, what about aggressive sours? You know, if you have aggressive sour, it's one argument they make to wanting a longer term of faring.

And I say, well, you unfortunately just call that sour because I mean, if she's a risk to you, you don't really want her. Um, but I think seven days, I think it's a fair, a fair period of time. It's what I would advocate for and what I stand by. I'm not a, I'm not for a zero faring pr I think sevens Very good answer.

Walt. Um, Rachel, you had another question for Walt while we Oh, no, I actually tied my question into a statement, sorry. No, no. Um, very quickly, any members, any other questions for Walt? Um, he's a great submitter, so, um, any other thoughts there? There appear to be none, Walt. Thank you so much, mate.

Um, I think Rachel's right. I think there's a lot to discuss, uh, in regards to Steve Abels bill, and, you know, given that it's not in the scope of this legislation, it would certainly be a MEUs argument to and discussion to have. So thank you for raising that and, um, we look forward to seeing you again.

Yes, I'll, I'll see you again, mark. I've tactually joined your party, so you'll be hearing a lot more from me now. Bless you. Thank you. Bloody legend. Well, you mate, always well and happy farming and talk soon. You too. Cheers, mark. Thank you. Cheer, we, um, we now have Sarah online, I believe.

Yes. Good morning Committee members, uh, thank you for this opportunity to speak to my submission. Can you hear me all right? Yes, Sarah, we lovely to speak. Great. So much for your Time. Um, great. Thank you. Um, thank you for this opportunity, uh, to speak in opposition to the animal welfare amendment Bill. Um, I understand that submissions are being taken as read, however, um, I only received notification of the speaking opportunity yesterday afternoon, so I will be referring to my submission.

Mm-hmm. Um, I wish, I wish to begin with a quote from Gandhi, uh, the greatness of a nation and its moral progress. And I would add that of a government can be judged by the way its animals are treated. Point one. In 2020, the high court declared the minimum standards and regulations that permit Faron crates and mating stalls for pigs were invalid and unlawful under the Animal Welfare Act 1999.

It's my understanding the court did so because the use of these crates did not meet the animal welfare its obligations to ensure physical health and behavioral needs of animals, including the opportunity to display normal patterns of behavior. The government's now proposing to change this law to make the use of these intensive confinement systems for pigs legal by embedding the cages into this act.

In my view, this undermines the court's judgment and the rule of law. Um, it's my understanding that dev, uh, point to, it's my understanding that developed countries with bans or restrictions on such methods include the entire eu, Sweden, Switzerland, Norway, and several American states.

The uk, um, are moving toward phasing out Faron crates, recognizing that they don't meet public expectations. And it's my view that New Zealand should adhere to similar standards of practice 0.3. It's my understanding that the economic analysis that have been undertaken to support the proposed amendment were conducted with significant input from New Zealand Pork New Zealand port, also provide much of the industry data.

It's therefore my view that this does not necessarily constitute independent economic evidence 0.4. Um, in my opinion, it's vital that best practice policy or regulation making, and in this case, this is the development of regulations that provide a good standard of care for farmed pigs.

Um, give it, it, it's, yeah, it's vital that best practice policy or regulation making gives equal weight to all evidence. Um, it's also, um, my opinion that there can be an inherent bias that elevates economic outcomes and evidence above other evidence that addresses sometimes less, um, ostensibly quantifiable or normative issues.

Uh, in this case, other evidence can include such things as verified evidence regarding animal welfare and wellbeing. And that's specifically provisioned for adequate space that's room to move, and behavioral needs that allow animals to express natural behaviors and also the prevention as far as reasonable or stress.

I think another important, um, uh, type of evidence is the public or consumer opinion, um, regarding a reasonable standard of treatment for farmed animals in the production of pork. This wider evidence, this wider range of evidence should, in my view, be weighted equally alongside any economic evidence.

On the seventh of this month, that's October, 2025. Marcelo Ferre, an associate professor of law at the Auckland Law School, had the following to say in an article for the newsroom, mating stalls are used to make artificial insemination of a sour an easier process for the inseminator.

And Faron crates are used when a sow is giving birth to and feeding her piglets, although there are some differences between them. They both achieve the same ends.
